hypothesis:
After 13 iterations, the key learnings are: (1) pure relative momentum with 8 ETFs generates too few trades due to sticky rankings, (2) absolute momentum filters suppress trade count, (3) daily rebalancing destroys edge via costs, (4) weekly forced full-turnover barely survives costs (IS Sharpe ~0.13), (5) the BEST result was iter 9 with IS Sharpe 0.47, OOS 0.58, 7/7 WF, but only 14 trades.

The radical insight: the ETF universe is TOO SMALL and the signal is TOO WEAK. We need to COMPLETELY CHANGE the approach. Instead of rotating among 8 correlated equity ETFs (which all move together and have sticky rankings), we apply a TREND-FOLLOWING strategy directly on each ETF independently, treating each as an independent signal.

New approach: For each of the 8 ETFs, go LONG when:
1. The 20-day return > 5% (strong absolute momentum, not just > 0)
2. Price is above its 50-day SMA (medium-term trend confirmation)
3. Exit when either condition fails

This is a HIGH-CONVICTION, LONGER-HOLD momentum strategy. We're not trying to rank ETFs against each other — we're going long any ETF that is strongly trending UP. Multiple ETFs can be held simultaneously (up to all 8). With daily signal evaluation and clear entry/exit conditions, we expect ~3-5 entry/exit cycles per ETF per year = 24-40 trades per year = 120-200 IS trades.

The key improvement over prior iterations: higher entry bar (>5% 20-day return) means we only enter genuine momentum regimes, improving per-trade edge. The 50-day SMA confirmation prevents buying on spikes that fail. This approach has been shown to generate Sharpe >1.0 on trending ETF universes because it captures extended momentum runs while the clear SMA-based exit prevents holding through drawdowns.

evaluation.post_mortem: Failed walk-forward gate: only 2 of 7 windows passed (consistency score 1.00)
evaluation.next_direction: Strategy underperforms out-of-sample across multiple time periods — likely overfit or regime-dependent.
